course.name: The Night Garden

spiritAnimal.name: Athena
spiritAnimal.symbolism: Wisdom, night vision, patience, silent flight
spiritAnimal.arc: A young owl who cannot see in the dark. Not blind — but unable to read the shadows, unable to find meaning in the silence between sounds. Through learning English, she gains night vision: the ability to understand what's unsaid, to read between lines, to hear the music beneath the words. By C2, darkness is where she's most powerful. The Night Garden is her home.

universalCharacter.name: El Maestro
universalCharacter.author: Luis Fernando Macías
universalCharacter.role: The MAIN AUTHOR of every game course. Not a guest — the philosophical spine. Macías's works provide the majority of literary peaks across all levels. The public domain authors (Lear, Carroll, Austen, Shakespeare, etc.) are GUEST authors who appear in specific destinations. Macías is present in 40+ destinations per course, same as in Spanish.
universalCharacter.works: Same literary excerpts adapted to English context

setting.description: Three continents, three worlds — the English-speaking world is vast

mundoDeAbajo.name: The Roots
mundoDeAbajo.location: British Isles — England, Scotland, Ireland, Wales
mundoDeAbajo.theme: Where English was born. Old forests, castles, moors, coastlines.

mundoDelMedio.name: The Crossing
mundoDelMedio.location: North America — East Coast to West Coast
mundoDelMedio.theme: Where English expanded. Cities, prairies, mountains, deserts, bayous.

mundoDeArriba.name: The Canopy
mundoDeArriba.location: Global English — Australia, India, Caribbean, Africa, Singapore
mundoDeArriba.theme: Where English lives now. Every accent, every culture, every truth.

A1.peaks: Playful, rhythmic, animal characters. Nonsense verse teaches sound before meaning.
A1.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: Edward Lear (1812-1888) — nonsense poetry, The Owl and the Pussycat
Rudyard Kipling (1865-1936) — The Jungle Book, animal voices

A2.peaks: Wordplay, curiosity, the joy of not understanding everything.
A2.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: Lewis Carroll (1832-1898) — Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
Beatrix Potter (1866-1943) — Peter Rabbit, nature and simplicity

B1.peaks: Social registers, irony, dialect awareness, humor with depth.
B1.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: Jane Austen (1775-1817) — Pride and Prejudice, social language
Mark Twain (1835-1910) — Huckleberry Finn, dialect and voice

B2.peaks: Complex structures, rhetoric, invention of words, poetic compression.
B2.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: William Shakespeare (1564-1616) — the master of English
Edgar Allan Poe (1809-1849) — precision, atmosphere, the weight of words

C1.peaks: Sophisticated register, interior monologue, rhetorical mastery.
C1.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: Virginia Woolf (1882-1941) — stream of consciousness, interior voice
Oscar Wilde (1854-1900) — wit, paradox, the art of conversation

C2.peaks: Mastery. Whitman's 'I sing the body electric' mirrors 'nombrar es crear.' Dickinson proves that fewer words can mean more. Yeats weaves myth into modern language.
C2.mainAuthor: Luis Fernando Macías (adapted to English context)
guestAuthors: Walt Whitman (1819-1892) — Leaves of Grass, naming the world
W.B. Yeats (1865-1939) — myth, symbolism, the power of naming

universal.author: Luis Fernando Macías (contemporary, licensed)
universal.role: MAIN AUTHOR — present at every CEFR level, 40+ destinations
universal.philosophy: 'To name is to create' — the thesis of every Babel Free course
universal.works: Same works as Spanish, adapted (not translated) to English literary context
universal.copyright: OWNED ✓ — by the author, daughter is the founder

spiritGuides.name: Robin
spiritGuides.role: The Robin — A1 companion, cheerful, always singing

spiritGuides.name: Badger
spiritGuides.role: The Badger — grumpy wisdom, British understatement

spiritGuides.name: Coyote
spiritGuides.role: The Coyote — American trickster, teaches through mistakes

spiritGuides.name: Whale
spiritGuides.role: The Whale — ancient songs, connects all oceans

humans.name: Sam
humans.role: 12-year-old from London, mixed heritage, notebook keeper (like Candelaria)

humans.name: Professor Blackwood
humans.role: Antagonist — wants to standardize English, eliminate dialects

humans.name: Grandmother Osei
humans.role: Elder — Ghanaian-British, keeper of oral traditions (like Doña Asunción)

humans.name: El Maestro
humans.role: The universal guide — appears by the river with a book

fromSpanish: Soul layer — breathing paddles, ecosystem backgrounds (100%)
Dictionary infrastructure — EN already has 1M+ words (100%)
Audio system + TTS fallback (100%, switch to EN voices)
Riddle quest mechanics (100%, new riddles in English)
Ecosystem background gradients (adapt colors for new biomes)
newRequired: Athena (Owl) character sheet — 3 life stages like Yaguará
Robin, Badger, Coyote, Whale character sheets
Sam, Professor Blackwood, Grandmother Osei character sheets
Composer: English character leitmotifs (reuse ecosystem ambience)

course.whyOwl: The owl sees what others cannot. English learners often feel blind — surrounded by a language they can hear but not understand. Athena learns to see in the dark. By C2, darkness is where she's most powerful.
course.tagline: Where words grow in the dark

designPrinciples.uniqueIdentity: Each language course has its own name, its own texture, its own kingdom. Spanish is 'El Viaje del Jaguar' (a journey). English is 'The Night Garden' (a place that grows). French could be a labyrinth. German could be a forge. The architecture is shared. The soul is unique.
designPrinciples.literaryTexture: Each course draws from a different literary tradition — not just different authors, but different KINDS of literature. Spanish has magical realism. English has nonsense verse, social comedy, gothic horror, stream of consciousness. French would have existentialism, surrealism, the essay. Each language teaches through the literary forms that THAT language invented.
designPrinciples.assetReuse: Illustrations: Athena (owl) is new. But ecosystem backgrounds, game UI, El Maestro's character sheet, CSS, music ambiences — all reused from Spanish. The Night Garden looks different but runs on the same engine.
designPrinciples.elMaestro: Macías appears in English too. 'To name is to create.' He appears by a river with a book, same as in Spanish. His excerpts are adapted to the English context but retain the same philosophy. He is the thread that connects all languages.
